title: Linseed oil wood polish
subtitle: A raw linseed oil and turpentine blend for feeding and protecting bare or dry wooden furniture
excerpt: Raw linseed oil penetrates wood fibres and provides a hardwearing natural finish. Mixed with pure spirits of turpentine, it is absorbed more readily than oil alone. This is for bare, oiled, or very dry wood; it is not suitable for lacquered or varnished pieces, and not for food-contact surfaces.
sourceNotes: Linseed oil in wood treatment: traditional woodworking reference. Raw versus boiled linseed oil in furniture care: raw linseed is slower to dry but safer on antique wood with fragile finishes; boiled (stand oil) dries faster. Pure spirits of turpentine as a thinner and penetration aid: Wood Finisher's Handbook, Sam Allen (1980). Safety note: linseed-oil-soaked rags are a spontaneous combustion risk and must be spread flat to dry outdoors before disposal.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. A 100 ml batch treats one medium chair or several smaller pieces.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stable; mix as needed or store in a sealed bottle. Linseed oil mixed with turpentine keeps at least 6 months.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use within 6 months. Linseed oil oxidises over time in the bottle. Discard if the blend becomes visibly thick, cloudy, or gummy. Store in a sealed container away from heat.

content.text: Linseed oil has been used to feed and protect bare wood for centuries. It penetrates the wood surface, adds depth to the grain, and hardens by oxidation rather than forming a surface film like a varnish. Mixing with pure spirits of turpentine (not white spirit, which is a harsher petroleum distillate) reduces the oil's viscosity and allows it to penetrate more deeply. The blend is suitable for bare, oiled, or very dry wooden furniture. It is not suitable for lacquered, varnished, or waxed surfaces, and not for any food-contact surface.

attrs.title: Rag disposal
attrs.body: Linseed-oil-soaked rags generate heat as the oil oxidises and are a spontaneous combustion risk. After use, spread rags flat outdoors to dry completely before disposing of them. Never bunch or pile oily rags.

items.prepNote: raw (not boiled) for use on antique or delicate wood
pure spirits of turpentine; not white spirit

content.text: Combine the linseed oil and turpentine in a measuring jug or directly in a bottle. Stir or swirl to mix. Work in a ventilated space; turpentine vapour is flammable and the fumes are unpleasant in a closed room.

Wipe the wood clean and make sure it is dry. Apply the blend sparingly with a lint-free cloth, working in the direction of the grain. Use less than you think you need; linseed oil that sits on the surface rather than absorbing forms a sticky residue. Leave for 1 hour, then wipe off any remaining surface oil with a clean cloth. Allow to dry fully before applying a second coat, which takes 24 hours or longer in cool conditions.

Spread used rags flat on a non-combustible surface outdoors until fully dry, then dispose of them. Folded or bunched oily rags can self-heat and ignite. This is not a theoretical risk; linseed-oil rag fires occur every year.
Store in a sealed bottle away from heat and sunlight. Use within 6 months. If the oil thickens or becomes gummy in the bottle, discard and make a fresh batch.
